items.explanation: The Local Group is our neighborhood galaxy cluster.

items.question: What is the approximate shape of the Milky Way?
items.explanation: Observations indicate a bar in the central bulge and spiral arms.

items.question: What is Pluto classified as today (common category)?
items.explanation: Pluto is a dwarf planet in the Kuiper belt region.

items.question: What is a comet’s icy tail generally pushed by?
items.explanation: Comet tails point away from the Sun as material is blown outward.

items.question: What is a ‘year’ on a planet?
items.correctAnswer: One full orbit around the Sun
incorrectAnswers: One full spin on its axis
items.explanation: Orbital period defines a planetary year.

items.question: What is an astronomical unit (AU) often used to mean?
items.explanation: 1 AU is a convenient Earth-centered distance scale in the solar system.

items.question: What is the Kuiper belt?
items.correctAnswer: A region of icy bodies beyond Neptune
items.explanation: Many trans-Neptunian objects reside in the Kuiper belt.

items.question: What is the Oort cloud (conceptual)?
items.correctAnswer: A distant spherical reservoir of cometary bodies
items.explanation: Long-period comets are often linked to distant icy reservoirs.

items.question: What is the Sun mainly composed of?
items.explanation: The Sun is a star powered by fusion of light elements.

items.question: What is the asteroid belt mostly between?
items.explanation: Many small rocky bodies orbit between Mars and Jupiter.

items.question: Which planet has the largest mass in the solar system?
items.explanation: Jupiter is the most massive planet.

items.question: Which planet is third from the Sun?
items.explanation: Order from the Sun: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars…

items.question: Why do planets stay in orbit around the Sun?
items.correctAnswer: Gravity provides the centripetal force for curved paths
incorrectAnswers: Planets are weightless so no force
items.explanation: Newtonian gravity explains closed orbits when speed and distance balance.

items.question: Why does Venus appear very bright from Earth?
items.correctAnswer: It is close to Earth and its clouds reflect lots of sunlight
incorrectAnswers: It emits its own light like a star
It is always behind the Sun
items.explanation: Venus’s thick clouds make it highly reflective (high albedo).

items.question: What is a Hohmann transfer (concept)?
items.correctAnswer: An efficient orbital maneuver between two circular orbits
items.explanation: Uses two engine burns to change orbital energy.

items.question: What is a gravity assist (flyby)?
items.correctAnswer: Using a planet’s gravity to change a spacecraft’s speed and path
items.explanation: Pioneer/Voyager missions used planetary flybys.

items.question: What is a rover mission’s main advantage on Mars?
items.correctAnswer: Mobility to study diverse rocks and terrain
items.explanation: Rovers extend in-situ exploration beyond a single landing site.

items.question: What is a space telescope’s advantage over many ground telescopes?
items.correctAnswer: It avoids much atmospheric distortion and absorption
incorrectAnswers: It is always closer to stars
items.explanation: HST and JWST observe above (or beyond) atmospheric limits for many bands.

items.question: What is deep space communication delay caused by?
items.correctAnswer: Finite speed of light over large distances
items.explanation: Mars signals can take many minutes one way.

items.question: What is low Earth orbit (LEO) used for?
items.correctAnswer: Many satellites and crewed stations because it is accessible
incorrectAnswers: Only solar observations outside Earth orbit
items.explanation: LEO is relatively close and requires less energy than deep space.

items.question: What is planetary protection?
items.correctAnswer: Policies to limit biological contamination between worlds
items.explanation: Prevents harmful forward/back contamination during exploration.

items.question: What is sample return (mission goal)?
items.correctAnswer: Bring physical samples back to Earth for lab analysis
items.explanation: Returned samples enable precise instruments on Earth.

items.question: What is the International Space Station (ISS) mainly for?
items.correctAnswer: Research and technology demonstration in microgravity
items.explanation: ISS supports long-duration human spaceflight science.

items.question: Why are planetary landings challenging?
items.correctAnswer: Thin atmospheres, high speeds, and precise timing
items.explanation: Entry, descent, and landing must dissipate huge kinetic energy.

items.question: Why do astronauts appear weightless in orbit?
items.correctAnswer: They are in free fall around Earth (microgravity environment)
incorrectAnswers: There is no gravity in space
Earth’s gravity ends at the atmosphere
items.explanation: Orbit is continuous free fall with high tangential speed.

items.question: Why do rockets need multiple stages?
items.correctAnswer: To shed mass and improve efficiency as fuel is burned
items.explanation: Staging drops empty tanks to accelerate payload better.

items.question: What does a star’s color roughly indicate?
incorrectAnswers: Mass only with no temperature link
items.explanation: Hotter surfaces emit more energetic light, shifting color.

items.question: What is a binary star system?
items.correctAnswer: Two stars orbiting a common center of mass
items.explanation: Many stars form bound pairs or multiples.

items.question: What is a black hole?
items.correctAnswer: A region where gravity is so strong that light cannot escape the event horizon
incorrectAnswers: A hole in the galactic disk
An empty bubble of vacuum only
items.explanation: Black holes are predicted by general relativity with an event horizon.

items.question: What is a main sequence star?
items.correctAnswer: A star fusing hydrogen into helium in its core (stable phase)
incorrectAnswers: A star that only reflects sunlight
items.explanation: Sun-like stars spend most of their lives on the main sequence.

items.question: What is a neutron star?
items.correctAnswer: An extremely dense remnant after a massive star’s core collapse
incorrectAnswers: A normal star like the Sun
items.explanation: Neutron stars can form from supernova cores under extreme gravity.

items.question: What is a red giant?
items.correctAnswer: A late-stage star that expanded after core hydrogen exhaustion
items.explanation: Low- to intermediate-mass stars swell when evolving off the main sequence.

items.question: What is a spectroscope useful for with starlight?
items.correctAnswer: Identifying chemical composition and motion via spectral lines
incorrectAnswers: Measuring a star’s exact size with a ruler
items.explanation: Spectral lines reveal elements and Doppler shifts.

items.question: What is absolute magnitude related to?
items.correctAnswer: Intrinsic brightness (compared at a standard distance)
incorrectAnswers: How bright a star looks from Earth only
items.explanation: Apparent magnitude depends on distance; absolute magnitude standardizes comparison.

items.question: What is parallax used for in astronomy?
items.explanation: Earth’s orbit provides a baseline for triangulating stellar distances.

items.question: What is the Sun’s general spectral class (common label)?
items.explanation: The Sun is often called a G2V star.

items.question: What powers most stars’ long-term energy output?
items.explanation: Stars shine by fusing light nuclei under extreme temperature and pressure.

items.question: Why do stars twinkle as seen from Earth?
items.correctAnswer: Earth’s atmosphere bends starlight as turbulence changes
items.explanation: Scintillation is an atmospheric effect for point sources.

items.question: What do reflecting telescopes primarily use to collect light?
items.explanation: Large mirrors can be supported more easily than huge lenses.

items.question: What does a refracting telescope use to bend light?
items.explanation: Early telescopes used objective lenses; modern designs vary.

items.question: What is a CCD/CMOS sensor’s role?
items.correctAnswer: Detect photons and record images electronically
items.explanation: Modern astrophotography relies on digital detectors.

items.question: What is a spectrograph on a telescope used for?
items.correctAnswer: Spreading light into wavelengths to study composition and motion
items.explanation: Spectroscopy is fundamental to astrophysics.

items.question: What is adaptive optics used for?
items.correctAnswer: Correcting atmospheric distortion in real time
items.explanation: Ground telescopes can rival space seeing in some bands with AO.

items.question: What is angular resolution?
items.correctAnswer: Ability to distinguish small separations on the sky
items.explanation: Better resolution separates closer objects.

items.question: What is chromatic aberration?
items.correctAnswer: Lens dispersion makes colors focus at different points
items.explanation: Achromatic/apochromatic designs reduce this in refractors.

items.question: What is interferometry in astronomy?
items.correctAnswer: Combining signals from separated telescopes to boost resolution
items.explanation: Very long baselines improve angular detail.

items.question: Why are larger apertures desired?
items.correctAnswer: They collect more light and improve resolution
items.explanation: Light gathering scales with area.

items.question: Why are many observatories on high mountains?
items.correctAnswer: Thinner, drier air reduces absorption and turbulence
items.explanation: Site selection improves image quality.

items.question: Why observe in infrared?
items.correctAnswer: To see through dust and study cooler objects
items.explanation: JWST’s infrared capabilities probe star formation and exoplanet atmospheres.

items.question: Why observe in radio wavelengths?
items.correctAnswer: Some objects emit strongly in radio (and dust is transparent)
incorrectAnswers: Radio waves don’t exist in space
items.explanation: Radio astronomy reveals cold gas, pulsars, and AGN.

items.question: In biology courses, anatomy often pairs with what complementary study?
items.explanation: Anatomy is structure; physiology is function.

items.question: What is a tissue?
items.correctAnswer: A group of similar cells working together
items.explanation: Epithelial, connective, muscle, and nervous are basic tissue types.

items.question: What is an organ system?
items.correctAnswer: Organs cooperating for a major function
items.explanation: Examples include digestive and cardiovascular systems.

items.question: What is an organ?
items.correctAnswer: Structures composed of multiple tissue types
items.explanation: Organs perform specific coordinated functions.

items.question: What is anterior in anatomical position?
items.correctAnswer: Toward the front of the body
items.explanation: Directional terms standardize descriptions.

items.question: What is cartilage?
items.explanation: Cartilage cushions joints and supports structures.

items.question: What is connective tissue’s hallmark feature?
items.explanation: Matrix includes fibers and ground substance.

items.question: What is distal?
items.correctAnswer: Farther from the trunk or origin
items.explanation: Used for limb structures.

items.question: What is epithelial tissue specialized for?
items.explanation: Epithelia protect, absorb, and secrete.

items.question: What is homeostasis?
items.explanation: Negative feedback often restores set points.

items.question: What is the cranial cavity?
items.explanation: The skull protects the brain within the cranial cavity.

items.question: What is the integumentary system’s main organ?
items.explanation: Skin protects, senses, and helps regulate temperature.

items.question: What is active transport?
items.correctAnswer: Moving substances against their concentration gradient using energy
items.explanation: Pumps like Na/K-ATPase perform active transport.

items.question: What is cytokinesis?
items.correctAnswer: Division of the cytoplasm after nuclear division
items.explanation: Cytokinesis completes cell division into two cells.

items.question: What is osmosis?
items.correctAnswer: Water diffusion across a selectively permeable membrane
items.explanation: Water moves toward higher solute concentration across membranes.

items.question: What is photosynthesis’s primary sugar product (common teaching focus)?
items.explanation: Light energy converts CO2 and water into sugars and oxygen.

items.question: What is the basic unit of life in many biology courses?
items.explanation: Cells are the smallest units performing life processes in organisms.

items.question: What is the main role of ribosomes?
items.explanation: Ribosomes translate mRNA into polypeptides.

items.question: What is the plasma membrane mainly described as?
items.correctAnswer: A phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins
incorrectAnswers: A solid wall of cellulose in animals
items.explanation: The fluid mosaic model describes membrane structure.

items.question: What is the rough endoplasmic reticulum studded with?
items.explanation: RER makes many proteins destined for secretion or membranes.

items.question: What membrane-bound organelle is often called the ‘powerhouse’ of the cell?
items.explanation: Mitochondria produce ATP through cellular respiration.

items.question: What organelle carries out photosynthesis in plant cells?
items.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and thylakoid membranes.

items.question: What organelle contains DNA in eukaryotic cells besides mitochondria?
items.explanation: The nucleus houses chromosomes and controls gene expression.

items.question: What phase of mitosis aligns chromosomes at the metaphase plate?
items.explanation: Chromosomes line up centrally before sister chromatid separation.

items.question: What are consumers?
items.correctAnswer: Organisms that obtain energy by eating other organisms
items.explanation: Herbivores, carnivores, and omnivores are consumers.

items.question: What are producers?
items.correctAnswer: Organisms that make organic matter from inorganic sources (e.g., photosynthesis)
items.explanation: Plants and many algae are producers.

items.question: What is a food chain?
items.correctAnswer: A linear sequence of who eats whom
items.explanation: Trophic levels transfer energy upward.

items.question: What is a food web?
items.explanation: Webs better represent complexity than a single chain.

items.question: What is an ecosystem?
items.correctAnswer: Biotic community plus its abiotic environment
items.explanation: Energy and nutrients flow through ecosystems.

items.question: What is biodiversity?
items.correctAnswer: Variety of life at genetic, species, and ecosystem levels
items.explanation: Higher biodiversity can support stability and services.

items.question: What is biomagnification?
items.correctAnswer: Increasing toxin concentration up food chains
items.explanation: Fat-soluble pollutants can concentrate in top predators.

items.question: What is carrying capacity?
items.correctAnswer: Maximum population size an environment can support long term
items.explanation: Resources and space limit sustainable population size.

items.question: What is niche (ecological)?
items.correctAnswer: An organism’s role and resource use in an ecosystem
items.explanation: Niche includes interactions, diet, and tolerances.

items.question: What is primary succession?
items.correctAnswer: Colonization of lifeless substrate (e.g., new rock)
incorrectAnswers: After a forest fire on soil
items.explanation: Pioneer species begin soil formation.

items.question: What is symbiosis?
items.explanation: Includes mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism.

items.question: What is the greenhouse effect (basic)?
items.correctAnswer: Atmospheric gases trap heat near Earth’s surface
items.explanation: Natural greenhouse effect warms Earth; human activities can enhance it.

items.question: What did Darwin propose as a mechanism for evolution?
items.explanation: Variation, inheritance, and differential survival drive adaptation.

items.question: What is a fossil record used for?
items.correctAnswer: Showing past life forms and transitions over geological time
items.explanation: Fossils provide historical evidence for evolutionary change.

items.question: What is adaptive radiation?
items.explanation: Often follows ecological opportunity.

items.question: What is an evolutionary trade-off?
items.correctAnswer: A benefit in one trait may cost another
items.explanation: Example: fast growth vs. strong defense.

items.question: What is convergent evolution?
items.correctAnswer: Similar traits evolve independently in different lineages
items.explanation: Wings in birds and bats are a classic analogy example.

items.question: What is fitness (evolutionary)?
items.explanation: Fitness is about passing alleles to the next generation.

items.question: What is gene flow?
items.explanation: Migration can introduce new alleles or homogenize populations.

items.question: What is genetic drift?
items.correctAnswer: Random allele frequency changes, especially in small populations
items.explanation: Drift can fix neutral alleles by chance.

items.question: What is homology?
incorrectAnswers: Similarity due to convergent evolution only
items.explanation: Homologous structures may differ in function.

items.question: What is speciation?
items.explanation: Reproductive isolation often plays a key role.

items.question: What is the modern synthesis?
items.correctAnswer: Integration of genetics with natural selection and population thinking
items.explanation: It connects Mendelian inheritance to evolutionary change.

items.question: Why is antibiotic resistance an evolution example?
items.correctAnswer: Selection favors resistant bacteria when antibiotics are misused
items.explanation: Heritable variation + selection changes population frequencies.

items.question: If both alleles are identical, the genotype is:
items.explanation: Homozygous means two copies of the same allele.

items.question: If two alleles differ, the genotype is:
items.explanation: Heterozygotes carry two different alleles at a locus.

items.question: What are genes made of (in most life on Earth)?
items.explanation: Genes encode functional products via transcription and translation.

items.question: What does PCR amplify?
items.explanation: PCR uses primers and a heat-stable polymerase.

items.question: What is a Punnett square used for?
items.explanation: It combines parental gametes combinatorially.

items.question: What is a dominant allele’s typical relationship to phenotype in simple models?
incorrectAnswers: It appears only if homozygous recessive
items.explanation: Dominance describes phenotype expression, not ‘stronger’ DNA.

items.question: What is a genotype?
items.correctAnswer: The set of alleles an organism carries
items.explanation: Genotype contrasts with phenotype.

items.question: What is a mutation?
items.explanation: Mutations can be neutral, harmful, or occasionally helpful.

items.question: What is a phenotype?
items.correctAnswer: Observable traits resulting from genotype and environment
items.explanation: Phenotype includes morphology, biochemistry, and behavior.

items.question: What is an allele?
items.correctAnswer: A variant form of a gene
items.explanation: Different alleles can produce different traits.

items.question: What is crossing over?
items.correctAnswer: Exchange of DNA between homologous chromosomes during meiosis
items.explanation: Crossing over increases genetic diversity.

items.question: Who is known for pea-plant experiments supporting particulate inheritance?
items.explanation: Mendel tracked discrete traits across generations.

items.question: What are bacteria?
items.explanation: Bacteria lack a membrane-bound nucleus.

items.question: What are extremophiles?
items.explanation: Studied in archaea and bacteria.

items.question: What are viruses?
items.correctAnswer: Infectious particles needing a host cell to replicate
items.explanation: Viruses are not classified as cells.

items.question: What is Gram staining used for?
items.correctAnswer: Classifying bacteria by cell wall properties
items.explanation: Gram-positive vs Gram-negative guides antibiotic choice empirically.

items.question: What is a biofilm?
items.correctAnswer: Communities of microbes embedded in a matrix on surfaces
items.explanation: Biofilms increase survival and tolerance.

items.question: What is a pathogen?
items.explanation: Pathogens include bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites.

items.question: What is a spore (microbiology context)?
items.correctAnswer: A resistant dormant form some microbes form
items.explanation: Endospores help some bacteria survive harsh conditions.

items.question: What is an antibiotic (classic definition)?
items.correctAnswer: A substance that kills or inhibits bacteria
items.explanation: Antibiotics target bacterial processes; misuse drives resistance.

items.question: What is horizontal gene transfer?
items.correctAnswer: Genetic material movement between organisms not parent-offspring
items.explanation: Conjugation, transformation, transduction shuffle genes in bacteria.

items.question: What is pasteurization?
items.correctAnswer: Heat treatment to reduce pathogens in food/liquids
items.explanation: Pasteurization improves safety without full sterilization.

items.question: What is sterilization?
items.explanation: Sterilization is stricter than disinfection.

items.question: Why is handwashing effective?
items.correctAnswer: It removes and disrupts microbes on skin
incorrectAnswers: It kills 100% of viruses instantly always
items.explanation: Soap and friction reduce microbial load.

items.question: How can derivatives apply to motion?
items.correctAnswer: Velocity is derivative of position; acceleration is derivative of velocity
incorrectAnswers: Acceleration is integral of position always
items.explanation: Kinematics links rates and position.

items.question: How can integrals apply to motion?
items.correctAnswer: Displacement is integral of velocity over time
items.explanation: Integrating rate accumulates change.

items.question: How is elasticity related to derivatives (basic)?
items.correctAnswer: Measures percent sensitivity using derivatives of demand
items.explanation: Elasticities use logarithmic derivatives in many forms.

items.question: How is marginal cost interpreted?
items.correctAnswer: Approximate cost of producing one more unit
items.explanation: Marginal cost is derivative of cost with respect to quantity.

items.question: What is arc length formula for y=f(x)?
items.explanation: Accounts for curve steepness.

items.question: What is center of mass idea?
items.correctAnswer: Weighted average position using density/mass distribution
items.explanation: Uses integrals in continuous objects.

items.question: What is exponential growth model dP/dt = kP solution form?
items.explanation: Proportional growth leads to exponentials.

items.question: What is logistic growth saturation idea?
items.correctAnswer: Growth slows as population nears carrying capacity
items.explanation: Logistic ODE includes limiting term.

items.question: What is probability density function property?
items.explanation: PDF integrates to probability.

items.question: What is related rates in geometry problems?
items.correctAnswer: Relate dr/dt, dh/dt, etc., using an equation
items.explanation: Differentiate constraint with respect to time.

items.question: What is surface area of revolution idea?
items.correctAnswer: Integrate 2π times radius times arc element
items.explanation: Rotating curves sweeps surfaces.

items.question: What is work as integral (1D constant force extension)?
items.explanation: Variable force integrates over distance.

items.question: If a derivative is positive on an interval, what is typically true of f?
items.correctAnswer: f is increasing on that interval
items.explanation: Sign of derivative relates to monotonicity.

items.question: What does f′(x) measure?
items.correctAnswer: Instantaneous rate of change of f at x
items.explanation: Derivative is the slope of the tangent line.

items.question: What does the second derivative test help find?
items.correctAnswer: Local max/min behavior at some critical points
items.explanation: Sign of f″ indicates concavity and helps classify extrema.

items.question: What is a critical point of f?
items.correctAnswer: Where f′ is zero or undefined (in domain context)
items.explanation: Critical points are candidates for extrema.

items.question: What is d/dx sin(x)?
items.explanation: Sine and cosine derivatives cycle.

items.question: What is implicit differentiation?
items.correctAnswer: Differentiate both sides treating y as y(x)
items.explanation: Used when y is not solved explicitly.

items.question: What is linear approximation near a?
items.explanation: Tangent line approximates function values locally.

items.question: What is related rates?
items.correctAnswer: Compute how quantities change using the chain rule over time
items.explanation: Link derivatives with respect to time.

items.question: What is the chain rule for f(g(x))?
items.explanation: Chain rule handles compositions.

items.question: What is the derivative of x^n (power rule)?
items.explanation: Power rule handles many polynomials.

items.question: What is the product rule for (fg)′?
items.explanation: Differentiates products without multiplying derivatives only.

items.question: What is the quotient rule pattern for (f/g)′?
items.explanation: Use carefully with denominator squared.

items.question: What does integration by parts derive from?
items.explanation: ∫ u dv = uv − ∫ v du.

items.question: What does ∫_a^b f(x) dx represent geometrically (when f≥0)?
items.correctAnswer: Area under y=f(x) from a to b
items.explanation: The definite integral accumulates signed area with sign for f below axis.

items.question: What is a Riemann sum?
items.explanation: Finer partitions improve approximation to the integral.

items.question: What is an antiderivative of f?
items.explanation: Antiderivatives differ by a constant.

items.question: What is improper integral?
items.correctAnswer: Integral over infinite interval or unbounded integrand
items.explanation: Use limits to define convergence.

items.question: What is net signed area?
items.correctAnswer: Integral counts area below axis negatively
items.explanation: ∫ can be negative if f is mostly negative.

items.question: What is the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1 idea)?
items.correctAnswer: Derivative of an accumulation function recovers the integrand
items.explanation: Connects differentiation and integration.

items.question: What is the average value of f on [a,b]?
items.explanation: Average value is integral divided by interval length.

items.question: What is the definite integral’s additivity on intervals?
items.explanation: Integrals split over adjacent intervals.

items.question: What is u-substitution?
items.explanation: Choose u to simplify ∫ f(g(x))g′(x) dx.

items.question: What is ∫ 1/x dx?
items.explanation: Logarithm arises from reciprocal integration.

items.question: What is ∫ x^n dx for n≠−1?
items.explanation: Power rule for integration with exception at −1.

items.question: What does it mean if limits from left and right disagree?
items.correctAnswer: The two-sided limit does not exist
incorrectAnswers: The two-sided limit is their average
items.explanation: Matching one-sided limits are required for a two-sided limit.

items.question: What does lim(x→a) f(x) = L mean (intuitive)?
items.correctAnswer: f(x) can be made arbitrarily close to L by taking x close enough to a
incorrectAnswers: f(x) must be defined at a
items.explanation: Limits describe approaching behavior, not necessarily the point value.

items.question: What is a removable discontinuity?
items.correctAnswer: A hole where a limit exists but the point value differs or is undefined
items.explanation: Redefining one point can sometimes ‘remove’ the discontinuity.

items.question: What is an indeterminate form?
items.correctAnswer: A limit form like 0/0 needing algebra or other tools
items.explanation: Indeterminate forms motivate techniques like L’Hôpital’s rule.

items.question: What is an infinite limit (informally)?
items.correctAnswer: Function grows without bound near a point
items.explanation: Vertical asymptotes often correspond to infinite limits.

items.question: What is continuity at a point a (basic idea)?
items.correctAnswer: lim(x→a) f(x) = f(a) with f(a) defined
items.explanation: Continuity links limits and actual function values.

items.question: What is lim(h→0) (f(a+h)−f(a))/h if it exists?
incorrectAnswers: The integral from a to h
items.explanation: This limit definition connects limits to derivatives.

items.question: What is lim(x→0) sin(x)/x (standard result)?
items.explanation: This classic limit underlies many trig derivatives.

items.question: What is lim(x→∞) 1/x (toward +∞)?
items.explanation: As x grows, 1/x shrinks toward 0.

items.question: What is the limit of a constant function?
items.explanation: lim c = c.

items.question: What is the squeeze theorem used for?
items.correctAnswer: Bounding a function between two others to find a limit
items.explanation: If two functions squeeze a third and share a limit, the third matches.

items.question: Why can lim(x→a) f(x) exist even if f(a) is undefined?
items.correctAnswer: Because limits depend on nearby values, not necessarily the point
items.explanation: Example: rational functions with a canceling factor.

items.question: What is Green’s theorem (idea)?
items.correctAnswer: Relates line integral around closed curve to double integral over interior
items.explanation: Connects circulation and curl in 2D.

items.question: What is Jacobian determinant used for?
items.explanation: Substitutions in multiple integrals need Jacobian factor.

items.question: What is Stokes’ theorem (idea)?
items.correctAnswer: Relates circulation around boundary to curl flux through surface
incorrectAnswers: Relates area to perimeter in all dimensions
items.explanation: Generalizes Green’s theorem.

items.question: What is a double integral?
items.explanation: Computes volume under a surface z=f(x,y) when f≥0.

items.question: What is a line integral of a scalar field?
items.correctAnswer: Integral along a curve with arc length element
items.explanation: Used for mass of a wire with density.

items.question: What is a partial derivative?
incorrectAnswers: Derivative with respect to all variables at once
items.explanation: Measures sensitivity to one input.

items.question: What is a triple integral?
items.explanation: Used for volume and mass with density.

items.question: What is a vector field?
items.correctAnswer: Assigns a vector to each point in space
items.explanation: Used for flow and force fields.

items.question: What is curl (basic meaning)?
items.correctAnswer: Measures local rotation of a vector field
items.explanation: Important in fluid and EM contexts.

items.question: What is divergence (basic meaning)?
items.correctAnswer: Measures net outflow of a vector field
items.explanation: Positive divergence suggests sources.

items.question: What is divergence theorem (Gauss) idea?
items.correctAnswer: Relates flux through closed surface to divergence volume integral
items.explanation: Used in Maxwell’s equations formulations.

items.question: What is the gradient ∇f?
items.explanation: Points in direction of steepest ascent.

items.question: What is Maclaurin series?
items.explanation: Useful expansions for e^x, sin, cos.

items.question: What is Taylor series idea?
items.correctAnswer: Represent a function as an infinite polynomial from derivatives at a point
items.explanation: Coefficients involve factorial denominators.

items.question: What is a geometric series ∑ ar^n convergence condition (|r|<1)?
items.explanation: Common ratio determines convergence.

items.question: What is a power series?
items.explanation: Power series define functions on intervals of convergence.

items.question: What is absolute convergence?
items.explanation: Absolute convergence implies convergence.

items.question: What is alternating series test about?
items.correctAnswer: Sign alternation with decreasing magnitudes toward 0
items.explanation: Helps prove conditional convergence in some cases.

items.question: What is an infinite series?
items.explanation: Convergence depends on partial sums.

items.question: What is conditional convergence?
items.explanation: Rearrangements can change sums for conditionally convergent series.

items.question: What is p-series ∑ 1/n^p convergence?
items.explanation: Harmonic series (p=1) diverges.

items.question: What is partial sum S_n?
items.explanation: Series converges if partial sums converge.

items.question: What is ratio test often used for?
items.explanation: Looks at limit of |a_{n+1}/a_n|.

items.question: What is the nth term test for divergence?
items.explanation: Terms → 0 is necessary but not sufficient for convergence.

items.question: What are atoms composed of (subatomic particles)?
items.explanation: Nucleus contains protons and neutrons; electrons occupy outer regions.

items.question: What defines an element’s identity?
items.explanation: Changing protons changes the element.

items.question: What is an ion?
items.correctAnswer: Atom or molecule with net electric charge
items.explanation: Gain/loss of electrons forms cations/anions.

items.question: What is an isotope?
items.correctAnswer: Atoms of the same element with different neutron counts
items.explanation: Isotopes have same Z but different mass numbers.

items.question: What is atomic mass unit scale based on?
items.explanation: Relative atomic masses compare to a standard.

items.question: What is electron configuration?
items.explanation: Aufbau rules build configurations.

items.question: What is electronegativity trend?
items.correctAnswer: Increases toward fluorine (top-right, excluding noble gases)
items.explanation: Electronegativity reflects attraction for bonding electrons.

items.question: What is mass number A?
items.correctAnswer: Protons plus neutrons in a nucleus
items.explanation: A = Z + N for a nuclide.

items.question: What is periodic trend for atomic radius across a period?
items.explanation: Effective nuclear charge pulls electrons tighter.

items.question: What is photoelectric effect significance?
items.explanation: Einstein’s explanation supports E = hf.

items.question: What is quantum number principal n?
items.explanation: Higher n means higher average energy/distance.

items.question: What is valence electron?
items.explanation: Valence electrons drive chemistry.

items.question: What is VSEPR theory about?
items.correctAnswer: Predicting molecular shape from electron pair repulsion
items.explanation: Minimize repulsion between electron domains.

items.question: What is a Lewis structure?
items.correctAnswer: Diagram showing valence electrons and bonds
items.explanation: Dots and lines represent electrons and bonds.

items.question: What is a covalent bond?
items.explanation: Common between nonmetals.

items.question: What is a pi bond?
items.correctAnswer: Side-by-side p orbital overlap above/below bond axis
items.explanation: Double bonds include one σ and one π (typical).

items.question: What is a sigma bond?
items.correctAnswer: End-to-end orbital overlap along the bond axis
items.explanation: Single bonds are often σ.

items.question: What is an ionic bond?
items.correctAnswer: Electrostatic attraction between cations and anions
items.explanation: Forms between metals and nonmetals often.

items.question: What is electronegativity difference used for?
items.correctAnswer: Judging bond polarity (ionic vs polar covalent)
items.explanation: Large ΔEN suggests more ionic character.

items.question: What is formal charge used for?
items.explanation: Minimizes charge separation when plausible.

items.question: What is hybridization (concept)?
items.correctAnswer: Mixing atomic orbitals to explain observed bonding geometry
items.explanation: sp, sp2, sp3 models match geometries.

items.question: What is hydrogen bond?
items.correctAnswer: Strong dipole interaction involving H bonded to N, O, or F
items.explanation: Important in water and biomolecules.

items.question: What is metallic bonding?
items.explanation: Explains conductivity and malleability.

items.question: What is resonance?
items.correctAnswer: Multiple valid Lewis structures averaging electron distribution
items.explanation: True bonding is a hybrid of resonance forms.

items.question: What distinguishes aldehyde vs ketone?
items.correctAnswer: Carbonyl at chain end vs internal carbon
items.explanation: Aldehyde: RCHO; ketone: RCOR’.

items.question: What element is the backbone of most organic molecules?
items.explanation: Carbon forms stable covalent frameworks.

items.question: What is a carbonyl?
items.explanation: Found in aldehydes and ketones.

items.question: What is a functional group?
items.correctAnswer: Atom grouping that determines characteristic reactions
items.explanation: Examples: OH, COOH, NH2.

items.question: What is a hydrocarbon?
items.explanation: Alkanes, alkenes, alkynes are hydrocarbons.

items.question: What is amide linkage?
items.explanation: Common in proteins (peptide bonds).

items.question: What is an alcohol functional group?
items.explanation: R–OH is an alcohol.

items.question: What is an ester linkage?
items.explanation: Formed from carboxylic acid and alcohol.

items.question: What is benzene (concept)?
items.correctAnswer: Aromatic ring with delocalized π electrons
items.explanation: Exceptional stability due to resonance.

items.question: What is carboxylic acid group?
items.explanation: Acidic proton on hydroxyl of carbonyl.

items.question: What is chirality?
items.explanation: Chiral centers have four different groups.

items.question: What is polymerization?
items.explanation: Addition and condensation polymerization exist.

items.question: How are elements generally arranged in the modern periodic table?
items.explanation: Mendeleev’s periodicity led to atomic number ordering.

items.question: What are alkali metals?
items.explanation: They readily lose one electron.

items.question: What are groups (columns)?
items.correctAnswer: Vertical columns; often similar valence electron counts
items.explanation: Group number relates to valence patterns for main groups.

items.question: What are halogens?
items.explanation: Halogens tend to gain one electron.

items.question: What are metalloids?
items.explanation: Metalloids are semiconductors in tech.

items.question: What are periods (rows)?
items.correctAnswer: Horizontal rows; principal quantum number increases
items.explanation: Period number relates to valence shell trends.

items.question: What characterizes noble gases?
items.correctAnswer: Low reactivity due to filled valence shells
items.explanation: He, Ne, Ar… are largely inert under normal conditions.

items.question: What is effective nuclear charge trend?
items.explanation: More protons attract valence electrons more strongly.

items.question: What is electron affinity (concept)?
items.correctAnswer: Energy change when an electron is added to a neutral atom
items.explanation: Trends help predict anion stability.

items.question: What is ionization energy?
items.explanation: Generally increases across a period.

items.question: Where are metals generally located?
items.explanation: Metallic character decreases across a period.

items.question: Where are nonmetals generally located?
items.explanation: Nonmetals tend to gain electrons.

items.question: What does a chemical equation balance?
items.correctAnswer: Atoms of each element on both sides
items.explanation: Mass is conserved in chemical reactions.

items.question: What is Le Châtelier’s principle?
items.correctAnswer: System shifts to counter applied stress
items.explanation: Change concentration, pressure, or temperature disturbs equilibrium.

items.question: What is a catalyst?
items.correctAnswer: Speeds a reaction without being consumed overall
items.explanation: Provides alternative pathway with lower activation energy.

items.question: What is a decomposition pattern?
items.explanation: Breaking a compound apart.

items.question: What is a synthesis reaction pattern?
items.explanation: Building a compound from simpler substances.

items.question: What is activation energy?
items.correctAnswer: Energy barrier to start a reaction
items.explanation: Reactants must collide with enough energy.

items.question: What is combustion of hydrocarbon typical products?
items.explanation: Complete combustion oxidizes carbon and hydrogen fully.

items.question: What is double replacement?
items.explanation: Ions exchange partners often in aqueous solutions.

items.question: What is equilibrium (dynamic)?
items.correctAnswer: Forward and reverse rates equal; concentrations stable
items.explanation: Macroscopic constancy with ongoing molecular process.

items.question: What is oxidation (electron view)?
items.explanation: OIL RIG mnemonic: oxidation is loss.

items.question: What is reduction (electron view)?
items.explanation: Reduction is gain (OIL RIG).

items.question: What is single replacement?
items.explanation: One element swaps into a compound.

items.question: How do you convert mass to moles?
items.explanation: Molar mass has units g/mol.

items.question: What are coefficients in a balanced equation?
items.explanation: Use ratios to relate amounts.

items.question: What is a mole?
items.explanation: Moles link mass to particle count.

items.question: What is dilution equation M1V1 = M2V2 about?
items.correctAnswer: Same moles spread into new volume
items.explanation: Useful for preparing solutions.

items.question: What is empirical formula?
items.explanation: May differ from molecular formula.

items.question: What is gas stoichiometry at STP link?
items.correctAnswer: 1 mol ideal gas ≈ 22.4 L at STP (historical standard)
items.explanation: Converts between gas volume and moles in ideal cases.

items.question: What is limiting reactant?
items.correctAnswer: Substance that runs out first and limits product
items.explanation: Theoretical yield is based on limiting reactant.

items.question: What is molarity?
items.correctAnswer: Moles of solute per liter of solution
items.explanation: M = mol/L.

items.question: What is molecular formula?
items.explanation: May be multiple of empirical.

items.question: What is percent yield?
items.explanation: Measures practical efficiency.

items.question: What is theoretical yield?
items.explanation: Assumes complete conversion of limiting reactant.

items.question: What is titration used for?
items.correctAnswer: Find unknown concentration via reaction stoichiometry
items.explanation: Equivalence point matches mole stoichiometry.

items.question: How many k-permutations from n distinct?
items.explanation: Order matters, no repeat.

items.question: How many permutations of n distinct objects?
items.explanation: All orderings.

items.question: How many subsets of an n-element set?
items.explanation: Includes empty set.

items.question: What does C(n,k) count?
items.correctAnswer: Ways to choose k items from n without order
items.explanation: Combination vs permutation.

items.question: What is bijection in counting?
incorrectAnswers: If A injects B, sizes equal always
If A surjects B, sizes equal always
items.explanation: Bijections preserve cardinality.

items.question: What is binomial coefficient C(n,k)?
items.explanation: Counts k-subsets from n.

items.question: What is derangement (idea)?
items.explanation: Classic counting problem.

items.question: What is inclusion-exclusion for three sets (pattern)?
items.correctAnswer: Add singles, subtract pairwise, add triple
items.explanation: Generalizes to many sets.

items.question: What is multiplication principle?
items.correctAnswer: If A has m ways and B has n ways, pairs have m×n ways
items.explanation: Sequential independent choices multiply.

items.question: What is n!?
items.explanation: Counts permutations of n distinct objects.

items.question: What is pigeonhole principle?
items.correctAnswer: If n+1 pigeons in n holes, some hole has ≥2
items.explanation: Guarantees collisions.

items.question: What is stars and bars (idea)?
items.correctAnswer: Count nonnegative integer solutions to x1+…+xk=n
items.explanation: Common ‘identical balls into bins’ model.

items.question: What is Eulerian trail idea?
items.explanation: Famous bridges problem.

items.question: What is Hamiltonian cycle idea?
items.correctAnswer: Visits every vertex exactly once and returns
items.explanation: Hard to decide in general.

items.question: What is a connected graph?
items.correctAnswer: Path between every pair of vertices
items.explanation: One component.

items.question: What is a cycle?
items.correctAnswer: Path that starts and ends at same vertex
items.explanation: Requires at least one edge in simple graphs.

items.question: What is a graph (discrete math)?
items.explanation: Models networks and relationships.

items.question: What is a path?
items.correctAnswer: Sequence of distinct vertices connected by edges
items.explanation: Walks allow repeats; paths are stricter in many texts.

items.question: What is a tree?
items.explanation: n vertices implies n−1 edges for finite trees.

items.question: What is adjacency matrix?
items.correctAnswer: Matrix indicating edge presence between vertices
items.explanation: Useful for computations.

items.question: What is bipartite graph?
items.correctAnswer: Vertices split into two sets with edges only across
items.explanation: No odd cycles in bipartite graphs.

items.question: What is chromatic number?
items.correctAnswer: Minimum colors to color vertices so adjacent differ
items.explanation: Graph coloring problem.

items.question: What is degree of a vertex?
items.explanation: Loops may count twice depending on convention.

items.question: What is planar graph (idea)?
items.correctAnswer: Can be drawn without edge crossings
items.explanation: Kuratowski’s theorem characterizes forbidden minors.

items.question: What are De Morgan’s laws (roughly)?
items.explanation: Negation distributes with swapping AND/OR.

items.question: What is a proposition?
items.correctAnswer: A statement that is either true or false
items.explanation: Propositional logic builds from true/false statements.

items.question: What is biconditional p ↔ q?
items.correctAnswer: p if and only if q
items.explanation: True when p and q share truth value.

items.question: What is conjunction p ∧ q?
items.explanation: True only when both are true.

items.question: What is contradiction?
items.explanation: Example: p ∧ ¬p.

items.question: What is disjunction p ∨ q (inclusive)?
items.explanation: True if at least one operand is true.

items.question: What is implication p → q?
incorrectAnswers: p if and only if q
items.explanation: False only when p true and q false.

items.question: What is negation ¬p?
items.explanation: Flips truth value.

items.question: What is predicate logic extension?
incorrectAnswers: Only truth tables for two atoms
items.explanation: Express statements like ‘for all’ and ‘there exists’.

items.question: What is proof by contradiction?
items.explanation: Shows the original statement must hold.

items.question: What is tautology?
items.explanation: Example: p ∨ ¬p.

items.question: What is valid argument (logic)?
items.correctAnswer: Truth of premises guarantees truth of conclusion
items.explanation: Validity is structural, not about real-world truth of premises.

items.question: What does modular a ≡ b (mod m) mean?
items.explanation: Congruence respects addition and multiplication mod m.

items.question: What is Bezout’s identity?
items.explanation: Shows gcd as integer combination.

items.question: What is Chinese Remainder Theorem about?
items.correctAnswer: Solving simultaneous congruences with coprime moduli
items.explanation: Combines residues uniquely modulo product.

items.question: What is Diophantine equation (idea)?
items.explanation: Classic discrete constraint.

items.question: What is Euler’s totient φ(n)?
items.correctAnswer: Count of integers ≤n coprime to n
items.explanation: Used in RSA and modular arithmetic.

items.question: What is Fermat little theorem (statement, prime p)?
items.explanation: Useful in modular exponentiation.

items.question: What is Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic?
items.correctAnswer: Every integer >1 factors uniquely into primes (up to order)
items.explanation: Explains prime importance.

items.question: What is a prime number?
items.correctAnswer: Integer >1 with no positive divisors other than 1 and itself
items.explanation: Primes are building blocks for integers.

items.question: What is divisibility a|b?
items.correctAnswer: b = ak for some integer k
items.explanation: Divisor divides without remainder.

items.question: What is gcd?
items.explanation: Euclidean algorithm computes gcd.

items.question: What is lcm?
items.explanation: lcm(a,b)·gcd(a,b)=|ab| for positive integers.

items.question: What is multiplicative inverse mod m?
items.explanation: Exists iff gcd(a,m)=1.

items.question: What is WLOG?
items.correctAnswer: Without loss of generality—reduce cases by symmetry
items.explanation: Simplifies proofs by picking representative case.

items.question: What is a conjecture?
items.explanation: Can later become theorem or be disproved.

items.question: What is a direct proof?
incorrectAnswers: Check finitely many cases only always
items.explanation: Most straightforward logical structure.

items.question: What is a lemma?
items.correctAnswer: Helper result used in bigger proof
items.explanation: Organizes complex proofs.

items.question: What is counterexample?
items.correctAnswer: Shows a universal claim is false
items.explanation: One counterexample disproves ∀.

items.question: What is existence proof nonconstructive?
items.correctAnswer: Shows something exists without exhibiting it
items.explanation: Classic in analysis and combinatorics.

items.question: What is if-and-only-if proof structure?
items.explanation: Equivalence needs ⇒ and ⇐.

items.question: What is mathematical induction for ∀n P(n)?
items.explanation: Domino principle for integers ≥ start.

items.question: What is proof by cases?
items.explanation: Ensures all possibilities covered.

items.question: What is proof by contrapositive?
items.explanation: Contrapositive is logically equivalent to original implication.

items.question: What is strong induction variant?
items.correctAnswer: Assume all smaller cases to prove next
items.explanation: Useful when P(n) depends on multiple predecessors.

items.question: What is uniqueness proof?
items.correctAnswer: Show at most one object satisfies conditions
items.explanation: Often assume two and derive equality.

items.question: What are disjoint sets?
incorrectAnswers: One is subset of the other
items.explanation: No shared elements.

items.question: What is Cartesian product A×B?
items.explanation: Builds planes like R×R.

items.question: What is a set?
items.explanation: Elements are unique; order not inherent.

items.question: What is cardinality for finite sets?
items.explanation: Size measure for finite sets.

items.question: What is complement of A (in U)?
items.correctAnswer: Elements in U not in A
items.explanation: Depends on universal set context.

items.question: What is inclusion-exclusion for |A∪B|?
items.explanation: Avoids double counting.

items.question: What is intersection A ∩ B?
items.correctAnswer: Elements in both A and B
items.explanation: Common membership.

items.question: What is power set P(A)?
items.correctAnswer: Set of all subsets of A
items.explanation: |P(A)| = 2^{|A|} for finite A.

items.question: What is set difference A − B?
items.correctAnswer: Elements in A but not B
incorrectAnswers: Elements in B but not A
items.explanation: Also written A \ B.

items.question: What is subset A ⊆ B?
items.correctAnswer: Every element of A is in B
incorrectAnswers: Every element of B is in A
items.explanation: Subset allows equality.

items.question: What is union A ∪ B?
items.correctAnswer: Elements in A or B or both
items.explanation: Combines membership.

items.question: What is ∅?
items.explanation: Contains no elements.

items.question: In Earth–space context, what is a satellite?
items.explanation: Moon is a natural satellite.

items.question: What causes seasons on Earth?
items.correctAnswer: Tilt of Earth’s axis relative to orbit
items.explanation: Axial tilt changes solar intensity per hemisphere.

items.question: What is Doppler shift for stars?
items.explanation: Redshift/blueshift indicates line-of-sight velocity.

items.question: What is a constellation?
items.correctAnswer: A named pattern of stars from Earth’s viewpoint
items.explanation: Patterns are cultural projections.

items.question: What is a light-year?
items.correctAnswer: Distance light travels in one year
items.explanation: Used for interstellar distances.

items.question: What is a lunar phase?
items.correctAnswer: Sun–Earth–Moon geometry changing illuminated fraction seen
incorrectAnswers: Moon emits its own light phases
items.explanation: Phases repeat monthly.

items.question: What is a meteor?
items.correctAnswer: Bright streak from meteoroid burning in atmosphere
items.explanation: Called meteorite if survives to ground.

items.question: What is an exoplanet?
items.explanation: Detected via transits, RV, imaging.

items.question: What is habitable zone (informal)?
items.correctAnswer: Region where liquid water could exist on a rocky planet
items.explanation: Depends on star luminosity and atmosphere.

items.question: What is lunar eclipse (basic)?
items.correctAnswer: Earth’s shadow falls on the Moon
items.explanation: Occurs at full moon alignment.

items.question: What is solar eclipse (basic)?
items.correctAnswer: Moon blocks Sun from a viewer’s location
items.explanation: Requires new moon near node alignment.

items.question: What is the ecliptic?
items.correctAnswer: Apparent path of the Sun on the celestial sphere
items.explanation: Planets roughly follow the ecliptic plane.

items.question: What is El Niño (concept)?
items.correctAnswer: Warm phase of ENSO affecting Pacific and global weather
items.explanation: Trade wind changes alter sea surface temperatures.

items.question: What is IPCC?
items.correctAnswer: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change assessments
items.explanation: Synthesizes climate science for policymakers.

items.question: What is La Niña?
items.explanation: Often opposite rainfall teleconnections.

items.question: What is albedo?
items.explanation: Ice and clouds increase planetary albedo.

items.question: What is carbon footprint (basic idea)?
items.explanation: Often expressed as CO2-equivalent.

items.question: What is climate normals?
items.explanation: WMO uses standard reference periods.

items.question: What is feedback in climate (example)?
items.correctAnswer: Ice melt lowers albedo causing more warming
items.explanation: Positive feedback amplifies change.

items.question: What is greenhouse effect (natural)?
items.correctAnswer: Gases trap heat; warms Earth’s surface
items.explanation: Without it Earth would be much colder.

items.question: What is mitigation vs adaptation?
items.correctAnswer: Reduce causes vs cope with impacts
items.explanation: Both are used in climate strategies.

items.question: What is proxy data in paleoclimate?
items.correctAnswer: Indirect records like ice cores and tree rings
items.explanation: Extends climate knowledge before instruments.

items.question: What is sea level rise driver (major)?
items.explanation: Climate change increases ocean heat and ice loss.

items.question: What is urban heat island?
items.correctAnswer: Cities warmer than surrounding rural areas
items.explanation: Surfaces and waste heat alter local climate.

items.question: What are Earth’s main compositional layers?
items.explanation: Layering reflects density and composition changes.

items.question: What is a fault?
items.explanation: Earthquakes occur on faults.

items.question: What is a mineral?
items.correctAnswer: Naturally occurring inorganic solid with ordered structure
items.explanation: Definite composition ranges are typical.

items.question: What is a volcano?
items.correctAnswer: Vent where magma reaches the surface
items.explanation: Can build mountains and islands.

items.question: What is erosion?
items.correctAnswer: Removal and transport of Earth materials by agents like water and wind
items.explanation: Different from weathering alone.

items.question: What is fossil record?
items.correctAnswer: Preserved remains/traces of past life in rock
items.explanation: Used to reconstruct ancient environments.

items.question: What is igneous rock?
items.correctAnswer: Formed from cooled magma or lava
items.explanation: Basalt and granite are examples.

items.question: What is metamorphic rock?
items.correctAnswer: Formed by heat and pressure transforming existing rock
items.explanation: Examples: marble, schist.

items.question: What is plate tectonics?
items.correctAnswer: Earth’s lithosphere divided into moving plates
items.explanation: Explains earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain building.

items.question: What is sedimentary rock?
items.correctAnswer: Formed from deposited and lithified sediments
items.explanation: Often preserves fossils.

items.question: What is the rock cycle?
items.correctAnswer: Processes that transform rock types over time
items.explanation: Weathering, melting, and uplift link types.

items.question: What scale measures earthquake energy release?
items.correctAnswer: Moment magnitude (successor to Richter for large events)
items.explanation: Seismologists use magnitude scales based on wave energy.

items.question: What causes tides (primary)?
items.correctAnswer: Gravitational pull of Moon (and Sun) on Earth
items.explanation: Tidal bulges follow astronomical forcing.

items.question: What drives surface ocean currents (major)?
items.explanation: Gyres circulate water in basins.

items.question: What is SONAR used for in oceanography?
items.correctAnswer: Mapping seafloor and water properties using sound
items.explanation: Sound propagates in water.

items.question: What is a mid-ocean ridge?
items.correctAnswer: Undersea mountain chain where new crust forms
items.explanation: Spreading centers create basaltic crust.

items.question: What is a wave crest?
items.explanation: Opposite of trough.

items.question: What is continental shelf?
items.explanation: Often rich in marine life.

items.question: What is coral bleaching (cause)?
items.correctAnswer: Stress (often heat) leads corals to expel symbiotic algae
items.explanation: Recovery depends on stress duration.

items.question: What is estuary?
items.correctAnswer: Where river meets sea; mixed salinity
items.explanation: Important nurseries for species.

items.question: What is photosynthesis zone in ocean?
items.correctAnswer: Sunlit euphotic zone where phytoplankton thrive
incorrectAnswers: Abyss with no light always productive
items.explanation: Light limits primary production depth.

items.question: What is salinity?
items.explanation: Average seawater ~35 ppt in open ocean.

items.question: What is thermohaline circulation idea?
items.correctAnswer: Deep circulation driven by density (temperature + salinity)
items.explanation: Connects global ocean basins.

items.question: What is upwelling?
items.correctAnswer: Deep nutrient-rich water rises to surface
items.explanation: Supports productive fisheries.

items.question: What is LEO?
items.correctAnswer: Low Earth orbit, relatively close and common for satellites
items.explanation: ISS orbits in LEO.

items.question: What is RTG (space power, concept)?
items.explanation: Used far from Sun.

items.question: What is Van Allen belts (basic)?
items.correctAnswer: Zones of trapped charged particles around Earth
items.explanation: Important for radiation exposure planning.

items.question: What is a launch window?
items.correctAnswer: Time range when mission can launch to meet targets
items.explanation: Orbit geometry and lighting drive constraints.

items.question: What is a space suit purpose?
items.explanation: Protects astronauts in vacuum.

items.question: What is docking vs berthing?
items.explanation: ISS logistics use both approaches.

items.question: What is escape velocity idea?
items.correctAnswer: Speed needed to break away without further propulsion
items.explanation: Depends on body mass and radius.

items.question: What is geostationary orbit concept?
items.correctAnswer: Orbit with period matching Earth’s spin (uses equatorial plane)
items.explanation: Useful for communications satellites.

items.question: What is microgravity in orbit often called colloquially?
items.explanation: Orbit is continuous free fall around Earth.

items.question: What is re-entry heating?
items.correctAnswer: Kinetic energy converts to heat in atmosphere
incorrectAnswers: Friction alone is the only explanation always
items.explanation: Thermal protection systems are required.

items.question: What is regolith?
items.explanation: Landing and sampling deal with regolith.

items.question: What is space debris risk?
items.explanation: Shielding and maneuvering mitigate risk.

items.question: What causes wind?
items.correctAnswer: Pressure differences make air flow from high to low
items.explanation: Coriolis effect steers large-scale flow.

items.question: What is a front?
items.explanation: Cold/warm fronts bring changing weather.

items.question: What is barometric pressure?
items.correctAnswer: Force from weight of air above
items.explanation: Falling pressure often signals storms.

items.question: What is dew point?
items.correctAnswer: Temperature air must cool to for saturation
items.explanation: Higher dew point feels more muggy.

items.question: What is humidity?
items.explanation: Warm air can hold more moisture.

items.question: What is hurricane (basics)?
items.correctAnswer: Tropical cyclone with organized thunderstorms and low center
items.explanation: Warm ocean waters fuel development.

items.question: What is precipitation?
items.correctAnswer: Water falling from clouds (rain, snow, etc.)
items.explanation: Requires condensation and growth of hydrometeors.

items.question: What is radar used for in weather?
items.explanation: Microwave pulses reflect off hydrometeors.

items.question: What is thunderstorm fuel?
items.correctAnswer: Moist unstable air and lifting mechanism
items.explanation: Updrafts build tall cumulonimbus clouds.

items.question: What is tornado (basics)?
items.correctAnswer: Violently rotating column of air touching ground
items.explanation: Often from supercell thunderstorms.

items.question: What is weather forecast uncertainty?
items.correctAnswer: Small errors grow because the atmosphere is chaotic
items.explanation: Ensemble forecasts express uncertainty.

items.question: What is weather vs climate (one-line distinction)?
items.correctAnswer: Weather is short-term; climate is long-term average pattern
items.explanation: Climate statistics summarize weather over decades.

items.question: What is COP (UNFCCC context)?
items.explanation: Country-level agreements.

items.question: What is attribution science?
items.correctAnswer: Linking extreme events/climate trends to causes
items.explanation: Uses statistics and models.

items.question: What is carbon budget?
items.correctAnswer: Cumulative CO2 emissions compatible with warming limit
items.explanation: Links emissions to temperature targets.

items.question: What is climate model?
items.correctAnswer: Equations simulating Earth system on computers
items.explanation: Used for projections and attribution.

items.question: What is climate sensitivity (rough idea)?
items.correctAnswer: How much warming results from a CO2 doubling (equilibrium concept)
items.explanation: Models and paleo evidence constrain estimates.

items.question: What is cryosphere?
items.correctAnswer: Frozen parts: ice sheets, glaciers, sea ice, permafrost
items.explanation: Melting affects sea level and albedo.

items.question: What is loss and damage (climate policy term)?
items.correctAnswer: Impacts beyond adaptation, especially in vulnerable nations
items.explanation: International finance discussions.

items.question: What is nationally determined contribution?
items.correctAnswer: Country climate pledge under Paris Agreement
items.explanation: Collective ambition updated over time.

items.question: What is ocean acidification?
items.explanation: Threatens calcifying organisms.

items.question: What is permafrost thaw concern?
items.correctAnswer: Releases carbon and methane; destabilizes ground
items.explanation: Positive feedback risk.

items.question: What is radiative forcing?
items.correctAnswer: Change in Earth’s energy balance due to a factor
items.explanation: Positive forcing tends to warm.

items.question: What is scenario (SSP/RCP idea)?
items.correctAnswer: Plausible future pathways of emissions and socioeconomics
items.explanation: Helps compare policy outcomes.

items.question: What is CITES (purpose)?
items.correctAnswer: Regulate international trade in threatened species
items.explanation: Lists species with trade controls.

items.question: What is IUCN Red List?
items.explanation: Categories from LC to extinct.

items.question: What is biodiversity hotspot?
items.correctAnswer: Region with high endemism and habitat loss
items.explanation: Prioritizes conservation investment.

items.question: What is bycatch?
items.explanation: Threatens marine megafauna.

items.question: What is captive breeding?
items.correctAnswer: Breeding endangered species in controlled settings for release
items.explanation: Used when wild populations critically low.

items.question: What is carbon offset (idea)?
items.correctAnswer: Compensate emissions via projects reducing/removing CO2 elsewhere
items.explanation: Quality varies; additionality matters.

items.question: What is corridor (conservation)?
items.correctAnswer: Linkage allowing species movement between patches
items.explanation: Mitigates fragmentation.

items.question: What is habitat fragmentation?
items.correctAnswer: Breaking habitat into smaller isolated patches
items.explanation: Reduces gene flow and survival.

items.question: What is poaching?
items.explanation: Threatens endangered species.

items.question: What is protected area?
items.correctAnswer: Legally managed region for nature conservation
items.explanation: Includes national parks and reserves.

items.question: What is rewilding?
items.explanation: May reintroduce apex species.

items.question: What is sustainable yield?
items.correctAnswer: Harvest rate that can continue without depleting stock
items.explanation: Fisheries management concept.

items.question: What is a food web’s role?
items.correctAnswer: Shows multiple feeding links and complexity
items.explanation: Better than a single chain for stability analysis.

items.question: What is an ecosystem service?
items.correctAnswer: Benefit people get from ecosystems (clean water, pollination…)
items.explanation: Includes regulating and cultural services.

items.question: What is biomagnification?
items.correctAnswer: Toxin concentration increases up food chain
items.explanation: Fat-soluble pollutants accumulate.

items.question: What is carrying capacity in ecology?
items.explanation: Depends on habitat quality.

items.question: What is coral reef ecosystem?
items.correctAnswer: Diverse marine community built by corals
items.explanation: Threatened by warming and acidification.

items.question: What is deforestation impact?
items.correctAnswer: Habitat loss and carbon release (among others)
items.explanation: Forest loss alters carbon and water cycles.

items.question: What is endemic species?
items.correctAnswer: Found only in a particular region
items.explanation: Islands often have high endemism.

items.question: What is invasive species?
items.explanation: Spread often human-assisted.

items.question: What is keystone species?
items.correctAnswer: Disproportionately large effect on community structure
items.explanation: Removal can collapse diversity.

items.question: What is restoration ecology?
items.explanation: Includes replanting and hydrology repair.

items.question: What is watershed?
items.correctAnswer: Land draining to a common waterbody
items.explanation: Managing land protects downstream water.

items.question: What is wetland value?
items.explanation: Wetlands are ecologically critical.

items.question: What is LCOE (idea)?
items.correctAnswer: Average cost per unit electricity over lifetime
items.explanation: Compares technologies approximately.

items.question: What is capacity factor?
items.correctAnswer: Actual output / maximum possible over time
items.explanation: Wind/solar vary with resource.

items.question: What is electrification (climate strategy)?
items.correctAnswer: Shift end uses to electricity (often paired with clean power)
items.explanation: Depends on grid decarbonization.

items.question: What is energy efficiency?
items.correctAnswer: Use less energy for same service
items.explanation: Key climate strategy.

items.question: What is fossil fuel?
items.correctAnswer: Energy from ancient organic matter (coal, oil, gas)
items.explanation: Burning emits CO2.

items.question: What is geothermal energy?
items.correctAnswer: Heat from Earth used for electricity/heating
items.explanation: Strongest in tectonic regions.

items.question: What is grid (electric)?
items.correctAnswer: System moving electricity from generation to consumers
items.explanation: Includes transmission and distribution.

items.question: What is intermittency challenge for solar/wind?
items.correctAnswer: Output varies with weather and time
items.explanation: Grids balance supply/demand.

items.question: What is nuclear fission (power context)?
items.explanation: Controlled chain reaction in reactors.

items.question: What is pumped hydro storage?
items.correctAnswer: Pump water uphill to store energy; release to generate
items.explanation: Large-scale grid storage.

items.question: What is renewable energy?
items.correctAnswer: Sources replenished on human timescales (sun, wind, hydro…)
items.explanation: Intermittency and storage are design challenges.

items.question: What is transmission loss?
items.correctAnswer: Some energy lost as heat in power lines
items.explanation: Higher voltage transmission reduces relative losses.

items.question: What is PM2.5?
items.explanation: Penetrates deep into lungs.

items.question: What is Superfund (US)?
items.explanation: CERCLA authorizes cleanup.

items.question: What is acid rain?
items.correctAnswer: Deposition of acidic compounds harming ecosystems/materials
items.explanation: SO2 and NOx are key precursors.

items.question: What is brownfield?
items.correctAnswer: Former industrial site with potential contamination
items.explanation: Redevelopment can revitalize cities.

items.question: What is eutrophication?
items.correctAnswer: Nutrient overload causing algal blooms and oxygen loss
items.explanation: Often nitrogen/phosphorus driven.

items.question: What is ground-level ozone?
incorrectAnswers: Protective ozone layer in stratosphere same as ground
items.explanation: Forms from NOx and VOCs in sunlight.

items.question: What is light pollution?
items.correctAnswer: Excessive artificial light disrupting ecosystems and astronomy
items.explanation: Affects circadian rhythms.

items.question: What is microplastics concern?
items.correctAnswer: Small plastic particles persist and accumulate
items.explanation: Enters food webs.

items.question: What is noise pollution effect?
items.correctAnswer: Stress and hearing harm for humans/wildlife
items.explanation: Chronic noise disrupts behavior.

items.question: What is nonpoint source pollution?
items.explanation: Common in agriculture and cities.

items.question: What is point source pollution?
incorrectAnswers: Spread runoff from many small sources
items.explanation: Easier to regulate than nonpoint.

items.question: What is thermal pollution?
items.correctAnswer: Heat discharged to water harming aquatic life
items.explanation: Power plants can warm receiving waters.

items.question: What are SDGs?
items.explanation: 17 goals with targets.

items.question: What is ESG?
items.explanation: Used in corporate reporting.

items.question: What is circular economy?
items.correctAnswer: Design to reduce waste by reuse/recycle loops
items.explanation: Contrasts with linear economy.

items.question: What is cradle-to-cradle (concept)?
items.correctAnswer: Design products for continuous material cycles
items.explanation: Aspirational circular design.

items.question: What is degrowth (idea)?
items.correctAnswer: Reduce material throughput in rich economies for sustainability
items.explanation: Debated strategy; focuses on well-being.

items.question: What is environmental justice?
items.correctAnswer: Fair treatment and meaningful involvement for all people
items.explanation: Pollution burdens often unequal.

items.question: What is greenwashing?
items.explanation: Consumers should verify claims.

items.question: What is life-cycle assessment?
items.correctAnswer: Assess environmental impacts across product lifetime
items.explanation: Craddle-to-grave or cradle-to-cradle.

items.question: What is sustainability (common definition)?
items.correctAnswer: Meet present needs without compromising future generations
items.explanation: Balances environment, economy, society.

items.question: What is sustainable agriculture?
items.correctAnswer: Farming that protects soil/water and productivity
items.explanation: Practices reduce erosion and nutrient loss.

items.question: What is voluntary simplicity?
items.explanation: Behavioral sustainability lever.

items.question: What is water footprint?
items.explanation: Includes blue/green/gray water concepts.

items.question: How many radians are in a semicircle?
items.explanation: A full circle is 2pi radians, so half of a circle is pi radians.

items.question: If a central angle and an inscribed angle intercept the same arc, how are they related?
items.correctAnswer: The inscribed angle is half the central angle
incorrectAnswers: The inscribed angle is double the central angle
items.explanation: An inscribed angle subtending a given arc measures half of the central angle subtending that same arc.

items.question: If parallel lines are cut by a transversal, how are alternate interior angles related?
incorrectAnswers: They are equal only for perpendicular lines
items.explanation: Alternate interior angles lie between the parallel lines on opposite sides of the transversal, and they match in measure.

items.question: If parallel lines are cut by a transversal, how are corresponding angles related?
items.explanation: Corresponding angles occupy matching positions at the two intersections, and parallel lines make those matching angles equal.

items.question: What converse angle fact can be used to prove two lines are parallel?
items.correctAnswer: If corresponding angles are congruent, the lines are parallel
incorrectAnswers: If the lines intersect once, the lines are parallel
If one angle is acute, the lines are parallel
If the transversal is horizontal, the lines are parallel
items.explanation: The converse of the corresponding angles theorem says equal corresponding angles imply the lines are parallel.

items.question: What distance property describes points on an angle bisector?
items.correctAnswer: They are equidistant from the two sides of the angle
incorrectAnswers: They are equidistant from the vertices of all nearby triangles
They are always on a perpendicular bisector
They have no special distance property
items.explanation: Any point on an angle bisector has equal perpendicular distance to each side of the angle.

items.question: What does the exterior angle theorem say for a triangle?
items.correctAnswer: An exterior angle equals the sum of the two remote interior angles
incorrectAnswers: An exterior angle equals the adjacent interior angle
items.explanation: When one side of a triangle is extended, the exterior angle matches the sum of the two nonadjacent interior angles.

items.question: What is each interior angle of a regular pentagon?
items.explanation: For a regular n-gon, each interior angle is ((n-2) x 180)/n. Substituting n = 5 gives 108 degrees.

items.question: What is the sum of the interior angles of a triangle in Euclidean geometry?
items.explanation: In the Euclidean plane, the three interior angles of any triangle always add to 180 degrees.

items.question: What is the sum of two complementary angles?
items.explanation: Complementary angles add to a right angle, so their measures total 90 degrees.

items.question: What is the sum of two supplementary angles?
items.explanation: Supplementary angles form a straight angle, so together they measure 180 degrees.

items.question: When two lines intersect, what is true about vertical angles?
items.explanation: Vertical angles are opposite each other at an intersection, and opposite angles formed by intersecting lines have equal measure.

items.question: If two figures are similar with scale factor k, how do their areas compare?
items.explanation: Area depends on two dimensions, so scaling all lengths by k scales area by k squared.

items.question: What does the shoelace formula compute?
items.correctAnswer: The area of a polygon from its ordered vertex coordinates
items.explanation: The shoelace formula uses ordered coordinate pairs to compute polygon area without subdividing it first.

items.question: What is a reliable strategy for finding the area of a composite figure?
items.correctAnswer: Break it into simpler shapes and add or subtract their areas
incorrectAnswers: Use one universal formula for every figure
items.explanation: Composite figures are usually solved by decomposing them into rectangles, triangles, circles, or other familiar shapes.

items.question: What is the area formula for a parallelogram?
items.explanation: For a parallelogram, area is the base multiplied by the perpendicular height.

items.question: What is the area formula for a rhombus using diagonals d1 and d2?
items.explanation: The diagonals split a rhombus into four right triangles, leading to one-half the product of the diagonals.

items.question: What is the area formula for a trapezoid with bases b1 and b2 and height h?
items.explanation: A trapezoid's area equals the average of the two parallel bases times the height.

items.question: What is the area formula for an annulus with outer radius R and inner radius r?
items.explanation: An annulus is the outer circle with the inner circle removed, so its area is the difference of the two circle areas.

items.question: What is the area formula for an ellipse with semi-axes a and b?
items.explanation: Ellipse area is pi times the semi-major axis times the semi-minor axis.

items.question: What is the area of a regular hexagon with side length s?
items.explanation: A regular hexagon can be divided into six equilateral triangles, giving the formula (3sqrt(3)/2) s^2.

items.question: What is the lateral surface area of a cone with radius r and slant height l?
items.explanation: The curved side of a cone unwraps into a sector, which leads to the formula pi r l.

items.question: What is the surface area of a sphere with radius r?
